  install nixos installer w/ full disk encryption how to

  Here's a practical, up-to-date (as of 2025–2026) way to install NixOS from the live USB with full disk encryption using LUKS (most common & well-supported approach).

  There are two main paths:

  Easiest — use the graphical Calamares installer (available in the plasma / gnome ISOs) → select "Encrypt" during partitioning
  Manual (recommended for control, flakes, Btrfs/ZFS, custom LVM, TPM auto-unlock later, etc.)
  Most experienced NixOS users do the manual method because it gives full control over the config from the beginning.

  Quick Recommendation Table (2025–2026 reality)
  Goal  Best Method in 2026     Approx. Difficulty
  Fastest / beginner    Graphical installer + Encrypt option    Easy
  Good control + LVM    Manual + LVM-on-LUKS    Medium
  Btrfs + snapshots     Manual + disko + Btrfs-on-LUKS  Medium+
  TPM2 auto-unlock (no password)        Manual + systemd-cryptenroll    Medium–Hard
  YubiKey / FIDO2 unlock        Manual + YubiKey PBA    Hard
  Modern Recommended Manual Method (LUKS + LVM or plain ext4/btrfs)
  Boot the latest minimal or plasma ISO → get internet → become root (sudo -i)

  0. Preparation
  # Check disks (be VERY careful!)
  lsblk -f
  nvme list    # or just ls /dev/nvme* /dev/sd*

  # Most common variables (ADJUST THESE!)
  DISK="/dev/nvme0n1"           # ← change this!!!
  HOST="mymachine"              # used in configuration.nix
  1. Partitioning (UEFI — most laptops/desktops 2025+)
  parted --script $DISK                    \
    mklabel gpt                            \
    mkpart ESP     fat32        1MiB 1GiB  \
    set 1 esp on                           \
    mkpart LUKS    1GiB         100%       # rest of disk
  → You now have /dev/nvme0n1p1 (EFI) and /dev/nvme0n1p2 (to be encrypted)

  2. Format EFI partition
  mkfs.fat -F 32 -n BOOT /dev/nvme0n1p1
  3. Create LUKS container (2025+ defaults are good)
  cryptsetup --type luks2 \
    --cipher aes-xts-plain64 --key-size 512 --hash sha512 \
    --pbkdf argon2id --iter-time 2000 \
    luksFormat /dev/nvme0n1p2

  # You will be asked for passphrase twice (choose strong one!)

  # Open it (choose any mapper name you like — "cryptroot" is common)
  cryptsetup open /dev/nvme0n1p2 cryptroot
  → unlocked device now lives at /dev/mapper/cryptroot

  4a. Option A – Simple (no LVM, just one ext4 or btrfs partition)
  # ext4 example
  mkfs.ext4 -L nixos /dev/mapper/cryptroot

  # OR better — btrfs (snapshots, compression, etc.)
  mkfs.btrfs -L nixos -f /dev/mapper/cryptroot
  Mount:

  mount /dev/mapper/cryptroot /mnt
  mkdir -p /mnt/boot
  mount /dev/nvme0n1p1 /mnt/boot
  4b. Option B – LVM on LUKS (still very popular in 2026)
  pvcreate /dev/mapper/cryptroot
  vgcreate vg /dev/mapper/cryptroot

  lvcreate -L  60G -n root vg     # adjust sizes
  lvcreate -L   8G -n swap vg     # or more/less
  # optional: lvcreate -l 100%FREE -n home vg

  mkfs.ext4 -L root /dev/vg/root
  mkswap     -L swap /dev/vg/swap
  # mkfs.ext4 -L home /dev/vg/home   (if you made home)

  mount  /dev/vg/root /mnt
  mkdir -p /mnt/boot
  mount /dev/nvme0n1p1 /mnt/boot
  swapon /dev/vg/swap
  5. Generate & tweak configuration
  nixos-generate-config --root /mnt

  # Now edit
  nano /mnt/etc/nixos/configuration.nix
  Important minimal changes for LUKS:

  { config, pkgs, ... }:

  {
    # ──── required for decryption ────────────────────────────────
    boot.initrd.availableKernelModules = [ "xhci_pci" "ahci" "nvme" "usbhid" "usb_storage" "sd_mod" ];
    boot.initrd.kernelModules = [ "dm-snapshot" ];   # if you use LVM + snapshots later

    boot.initrd.luks.devices."cryptroot" = {
      device = "/dev/nvme0n1p2";   # or "/dev/disk/by-uuid/…" (more robust)
      # fallbackToPassword = true; # optional
    };

    # If using LVM
    boot.initrd.luks.devices."cryptroot".preLVM = true;   # most people need this

    fileSystems."/" = {
      device = "/dev/vg/root";     # or "/dev/mapper/cryptroot" if no LVM
      fsType = "ext4";             # or "btrfs"
    };

    fileSystems."/boot" = {
      device = "/dev/nvme0n1p1";
      fsType = "vfat";
    };

    swapDevices = [ { device = "/dev/vg/swap"; } ];   # if you made swap

    # Recommended 2025+ additions
    boot.loader.systemd-boot.enable = true;
    boot.loader.efi.canTouchEfiVariables = true;

    # networking, timezone, users, desktop, etc...
  }
  6. Install
  nixos-install --no-root-passwd   # or with --root-password if you want

  # Reboot (remove USB)

  Quick disko + flakes modern style (very popular 2025–2026)
  Many people now use https://github.com/nix-community/disko

  Example minimal disko config → put in disko-config.nix → run

  nix --extra-experimental-features "nix-command flakes" \
    run github:nix-community/disko -- --mode disko disko-config.nix

  Most common gotchas in 2026:

  Wrong UUID in boot.initrd.luks.devices
  Missing kernel modules → no decryption prompt appears
  preLVM = true; needed when using LVM
  For TPM auto-unlock → add systemd-cryptenroll step after first boot
  Web page favicon
  35 sources

• There isn’t a current git diff in this workspace.

  The debug: /dev/nvme0n1p1 is not an HFS+ partition: exiting line is very likely harmless probe noise, not the real failure. That exact wording matches os-prober trying its macOS
  checker against your EFI partition. nvme0n1p1 is usually the EFI partition, which should be vfat, not HFS+.

  If you already created the encrypted partition and want to keep it, do not run any of these again on that disk: mklabel, mkpart, mkfs.*, luksFormat. Those would overwrite it.

  Use the existing layout instead:

  lsblk -f
  blkid

  # assuming the encrypted partition you want to keep is p2
  cryptsetup open /dev/nvme0n1p2 cryptroot

  # if cryptroot contains a filesystem directly:
  mount /dev/mapper/cryptroot /mnt

  # if cryptroot contains LVM instead:
  vgchange -ay
  mount /dev/vg/root /mnt
  swapon /dev/vg/swap   # if you have swap

  Then mount the existing EFI partition without touching it:

  mkdir -p /mnt/boot
  mount /dev/nvme0n1p1 /mnt/boot
  nixos-generate-config --root /mnt

  Your NixOS config should reference the existing encrypted partition, preferably by UUID:

  boot.loader.systemd-boot.enable = true;
  boot.loader.efi.canTouchEfiVariables = true;

  boot.initrd.luks.devices.cryptroot.device =
    "/dev/disk/by-uuid/<UUID-of-nvme0n1p2>";

  fileSystems."/boot" = {
    device = "/dev/disk/by-uuid/<UUID-of-nvme0n1p1>";
    fsType = "vfat";
  };

  Then set / to either:

  - /dev/mapper/cryptroot if the LUKS device holds the filesystem directly
  - /dev/vg/root if you used LVM inside LUKS

  If the partition you want to “keep” is macOS FileVault/APFS, that is different: NixOS cannot install inside that encrypted macOS partition. In that case, keep it untouched and
  create a separate partition for NixOS, reusing only the EFI partition.

  If you paste lsblk -f and blkid, I can map your exact partitions and give you the precise configuration.nix entries.

  Sources: NixOS manual (https://nixos.org/nixos/manual/index.html), NixOS Wiki: Full Disk Encryption (https://nixos.wiki/wiki/Full_Disk_Encryption)

input.content:
# Debug: `/dev/nvme0n1p1 is not an HFS+ partition: exiting`

## Context

PR #1105 updates `named-hosts/matic/hardware-configuration.nix` to reflect full-disk LUKS encryption. During the NixOS bootstrap install on matic, the debug message `debug: /dev/nvme0n1p1 is not an HFS+ partition: exiting` appeared. User wants to know why.

## Root Cause

**Source**: `os-prober` — runs automatically when `nixos-generate-config` scans the disk during installation.

**Why it prints this**: os-prober includes a macOS detector probe that:
1. Iterates every partition on the disk
2. Checks if it is an HFS+ filesystem (macOS format)
3. If not, prints `debug: <device> is not an HFS+ partition: exiting` and moves on

`/dev/nvme0n1p1` is matic's EFI/boot partition (FAT32/vfat, UUID `D849-552A`), not macOS HFS+. The probe correctly rejects it and exits.

That's a `nixos-rebuild switch` failure. The `switch-to-configuration` error alone doesn't say much — the actual cause is usually in the lines above it.

Can you share the full output of the rebuild command? Something like:

```
sudo nixos-rebuild switch --flake .#matic 2>&1 | tail -50
```

Common causes with a LUKS hardware-config change like this PR:
- **Mismatched LUKS UUID** — the UUID in `boot.initrd.luks.devices` doesn't match the actual partition
- **Missing initrd kernel modules** — `usb_storage` was removed in this PR, though unlikely to cause this specific error
- **systemd service activation failure** — unrelated to LUKS, something else in the config failing during switch

The full error log will pinpoint which unit or activation step failed.

# Fix: GRUB installing on matic despite systemd-boot config

## Context

`make switch` on matic fails with:
```
installing the GRUB 2 boot loader on /dev/sda...
grub-install: warning: Attempting to install GRUB to a disk with multiple partition labels. This is not supported yet..
Command 'systemd-run' ... returned non-zero exit status 1.
```

The matic config (`named-hosts/matic/default.nix:41`) correctly sets `boot.loader.systemd-boot.enable = true`, but GRUB is **also** running and trying to install to `/dev/sda` (likely an SD card reader or USB device on the Framework 13 — not the NVMe boot drive).

## Root Cause

In NixOS, `boot.loader.grub.enable` defaults to `!config.boot.isContainer` (i.e. `true` on real hardware). While the systemd-boot module sets `boot.loader.grub.enable = mkDefault false`, this can be overridden. The matic config never explicitly disables GRUB, so it ends up enabled alongside systemd-boot.

GRUB then:
1. Enables `os-prober` (default behavior) — producing the harmless HFS+/BeFS debug noise
2. Tries to install to `/dev/sda` which has conflicting partition labels — **this is the actual failure**

The `hosts/nixos/default.nix` (generic NixOS config, NOT used by matic) has explicit GRUB settings — but matic doesn't import it. The issue is purely the NixOS GRUB module default.

## Fix

**File**: `named-hosts/matic/default.nix`

Add one line after `boot.loader.systemd-boot.enable = true;` (line 41):

```nix
boot.loader.grub.enable = false;
```

This explicitly disables GRUB, which:
- Stops the failed `grub-install` to `/dev/sda`
- Eliminates all the os-prober debug noise
- Leaves systemd-boot as the sole bootloader (correct for UEFI + LUKS on NVMe)

## Verification

After the edit, on matic run:
```bash
make build && make switch
```

Expected: no GRUB output, no os-prober noise, clean systemd-boot activation.

# Debug: `/dev/nvme0n1p1 is not an HFS+ partition: exiting`

## Context

PR #1105 updates `named-hosts/matic/hardware-configuration.nix` to reflect full-disk LUKS encryption. During the NixOS bootstrap install on matic, the debug message `debug: /dev/nvme0n1p1 is not an HFS+ partition: exiting` appeared. User wants to know why.

## Root Cause

**Source**: `os-prober` — runs automatically when `nixos-generate-config` scans the disk during installation.

**Why it prints this**: os-prober includes a macOS detector probe that:
1. Iterates every partition on the disk
2. Checks if it is an HFS+ filesystem (macOS format)
3. If not, prints `debug: <device> is not an HFS+ partition: exiting` and moves on

`/dev/nvme0n1p1` is matic's EFI/boot partition (FAT32/vfat, UUID `D849-552A`), not macOS HFS+. The probe correctly rejects it and exits.

**Severity**: **Completely harmless** — it is informational probe noise, not an error. Installation proceeds normally.

## Why It Won't Recur at Boot

matic uses `systemd-boot` (`boot.loader.systemd-boot.enable = true`), not GRUB. `os-prober` is only invoked by GRUB to find other operating systems for the boot menu. With systemd-boot, os-prober never runs at boot time — it only ran during `nixos-generate-config` at install time.

## PR #1105 Hardware Config Changes (Correct)

| | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| `/` device | `/dev/disk/by-uuid/019b9ecb-...` (plain ext4) | `/dev/mapper/luks-4a2ddfc4-...` (ext4 over LUKS) |
| LUKS device | — | `/dev/disk/by-uuid/4a2ddfc4-...` |
| `/boot` UUID | `4070-0CFA` | `D849-552A` |

The LUKS configuration is correct NixOS idiom. No issues.

## Conclusion

**No code changes needed.** The message is expected os-prober noise from `nixos-generate-config`. The hardware configuration is correct. PR #1105 is safe to merge.

# Fix: GRUB installing on matic despite systemd-boot config

## Context

`make switch` on matic fails with:
```
installing the GRUB 2 boot loader on /dev/sda...
grub-install: warning: Attempting to install GRUB to a disk with multiple partition labels. This is not supported yet..
Command 'systemd-run' ... returned non-zero exit status 1.
```

The matic config (`named-hosts/matic/default.nix:41`) correctly sets `boot.loader.systemd-boot.enable = true`, but GRUB is **also** running and trying to install to `/dev/sda` (likely an SD card reader or USB device on the Framework 13 — not the NVMe boot drive).

## Root Cause

In NixOS, `boot.loader.grub.enable` defaults to `!config.boot.isContainer` (i.e. `true` on real hardware). While the systemd-boot module sets `boot.loader.grub.enable = mkDefault false`, this can be overridden. The matic config never explicitly disables GRUB, so it ends up enabled alongside systemd-boot.

GRUB then:
1. Enables `os-prober` (default behavior) — producing the harmless HFS+/BeFS debug noise
2. Tries to install to `/dev/sda` which has conflicting partition labels — **this is the actual failure**

The `hosts/nixos/default.nix` (generic NixOS config, NOT used by matic) has explicit GRUB settings — but matic doesn't import it. The issue is purely the NixOS GRUB module default.

## Fix

**File**: `named-hosts/matic/default.nix`

Add one line after `boot.loader.systemd-boot.enable = true;` (line 41):

```nix
boot.loader.grub.enable = false;
```

This explicitly disables GRUB, which:
- Stops the failed `grub-install` to `/dev/sda`
- Eliminates all the os-prober debug noise
- Leaves systemd-boot as the sole bootloader (correct for UEFI + LUKS on NVMe)

## Verification

After the edit, on matic run:
```bash
make build && make switch
```

Expected: no GRUB output, no os-prober noise, clean systemd-boot activation.

content.text:
That's a common NixOS issue during major config switches. Use `boot` instead of `switch` to install without activating, then reboot:

```bash
sudo nixos-rebuild boot --flake .#matic --impure && sudo reboot
```

message.content: how to enable or authorize fingerprint

content.text:
Your matic config already has `services.fprintd.enable = true;` (`named-hosts/matic/default.nix:109`). You just need to enroll your fingerprint:

```bash
fprintd-enroll
```

Follow the prompts to scan your finger multiple times. To enroll additional fingers:

```bash
fprintd-enroll -f right-index-finger
fprintd-enroll -f left-index-finger
```

To verify it works:

```bash
fprintd-verify
```

After enrollment, fingerprint auth will work for `sudo`, login, etc. via PAM automatically.

message.content: will it work for launch disk de-encryption?

content.text:
No — LUKS disk decryption happens in the initrd (before the OS boots), and fprintd isn't available at that stage. The LUKS passphrase prompt is handled by the kernel/systemd in early boot, long before userspace services like fprintd start.

For passwordless disk unlock on NixOS, your options are:

1. **TPM2 auto-unlock** (most common on Framework laptops):
   ```bash
   sudo systemd-cryptenroll --tpm2-device=auto /dev/nvme0n1p2
   ```
   Then add to your NixOS config:
   ```nix
   boot.initrd.systemd.enable = true;
   boot.initrd.luks.devices."luks-4a2ddfc4-1a40-4e18-99f5-250baf72b4ac".cryptTabExtraOpts = [ "tpm2-device=auto" ];
   ```

2. **FIDO2/YubiKey** — tap a hardware key instead of typing a passphrase

Fingerprint readers require a full userspace stack (USB drivers, libfprint, dbus) that isn't practical to load in the initrd.

Want me to set up TPM2 auto-unlock in the matic config?

With TPM2 auto-unlock, the TPM chip on your Framework board stores a LUKS key sealed to the boot state. On boot:

1. Systemd in initrd asks the TPM2 to release the key
2. TPM2 checks that the boot chain is unmodified (firmware, bootloader, kernel, initrd)
3. If everything matches, it auto-decrypts — **no passphrase prompt**
4. If the boot chain changed (firmware update, different kernel, etc.), TPM2 refuses and you **fall back to the passphrase**

**What this means practically:**
- Normal boot: instant, no password
- After kernel/firmware update: you'll need to type your passphrase once, then re-enroll
- If someone pulls the NVMe and puts it in another machine: TPM won't release the key, data stays encrypted
- If someone tampers with the bootloader: TPM refuses, passphrase required

**Trade-off vs passphrase-only:**
- Convenience: much better (no typing on every boot)
- Security: protects against disk theft, but if someone has physical access to *your* machine while it's off and the boot chain is intact, they could boot it. You'd rely on your login password at that point
- Some people combine TPM + PIN for both convenience and stronger security: `systemd-cryptenroll --tpm2-device=auto --tpm2-with-pin=yes`

Want me to add TPM2 auto-unlock (with or without PIN) to the matic config?

With or without PIN?

- **Without PIN**: fully automatic boot, no input needed
- **With PIN**: type a short PIN instead of the full passphrase (more secure)

Either way, let me add the config. I'll go without PIN for now (you can add PIN later with `systemd-cryptenroll --tpm2-with-pin=yes`).

For troubleshooting and frequently asked questions, see [FAQ.md](./FAQ.md).

## NixOS (matic - Framework 13 AMD AI 300)

### Full Disk Encryption (LUKS + TPM2)

The root partition is encrypted with LUKS2. TPM2 auto-unlock is configured so no passphrase is needed on boot.

After a fresh install or kernel update, enroll the TPM2 key:

```bash
sudo systemd-cryptenroll --tpm2-device=auto /dev/nvme0n1p2
```

If the boot chain changes (firmware/kernel update), TPM2 will refuse and fall back to the passphrase. Re-enroll after booting with the passphrase.

### Building and Switching

```bash
make build HOST=matic && make switch HOST=matic
```

Or use `boot` instead of `switch` for major config changes (avoids dbus reload issues):

```bash
sudo nixos-rebuild boot --flake .#matic --impure && sudo reboot
```

## Credits
